P2-001.title: Is LinkedIn Failing Gen Z?
P2-001.author: BIXA Research
P2-001.excerpt: 96% of Gen Z users (ages 19-24) rarely or never use LinkedIn despite over 50% having accounts. Most accounts were created under pressure from parents or universities, not by choice. Gen Z job-seekers report feeling overwhelmed comparing their minimal experience to established professionals. One user noted seeing 'robust profiles of highly experienced individuals' while struggling to frame summer jobs and internships as meaningful professional experience. Young users view LinkedIn as 'purely transactional' — they enter, search for jobs with 'surgical precision,' then leave. They see no functional difference between LinkedIn and other job boards like Indeed. Gen Z doesn't recognize LinkedIn as social media and avoids its news feed entirely, despite spending 3+ hours daily on other platforms.
P2-001.relevance: Anchor evidence for Gen Z disengagement / mismatch pain point and 'identity-tied-to-past-roles' problem for early-career users.
P2-001.credibility: medium — 2020 study, sample method not fully transparent, but specific statistic is widely cited and pattern is consistent with later evidence.

P2-002.title: I Gave Up on LinkedIn. Should You?
P2-002.author: Karola Karlson (marketing consultant)
P2-002.excerpt: 54% of LinkedIn long-form posts were AI-generated as of October 2024. Platform 'drowning in slop' — cheap, copied content rewritten by AI. Criticizes users without real experience framing themselves as industry experts after 10 minutes daily. Thousands comment 'prompt' seeking cheatsheets without questioning authors' actual achievements. Observed viral posts claiming ability to create '20 GREAT ads in 10 mins WITH JUST VIBES'. As an experienced marketer, assessed these AI-generated creatives as lacking value proposition or CTA. ZERO consulting projects or course sales originated from LinkedIn as first touchpoint over 6 months. All revenue came through referrals and newsletter subscribers. Giving up on LinkedIn for 3 months minimum, keeping account only for professional networking and DMs.
P2-002.relevance: First-hand marketer testimonial: AI-slop saturation + zero ROI over 6 months + selective abandonment, not full quit. Important nuance.
P2-002.credibility: high — author is real practitioner with named clients, dated and specific.

P2-003.title: What Happened to Polywork? An Eye-Opening Postmortem
P2-003.author: Prompt-Led Product (Substack)
P2-003.excerpt: Five reasons Polywork failed: (1) Network effect underestimation — couldn't achieve critical mass; (2) Weak differentiation — 'Unique features aren't enough; your product needs a clear, compelling value proposition'; (3) Confusing pivot — switched from LinkedIn competitor to website builder, alienated early adopters; (4) Funding pressure — $13M Series A pushed unsustainable growth; (5) Market timing — launch during tech fatigue. Quoted user feedback: Antonio Avalos: 'people don't want many websites or portfolios to visit just a standardized view in a trusty place.' Benedikt Kantus: 'They never provided sufficient additional value over LinkedIn.' Founder Peter Johnston on X (Dec 2024): 'after 3.5 years, I made the call to shut @Polywork down and return the remaining capital to investors.'
P2-003.relevance: Most thorough postmortem of a serious LinkedIn challenger; key data point on why migration is structurally hard and why complaining ≠ willing to leave.
P2-003.credibility: high — multiple primary-source quotes from founder and users, well-reasoned analysis.

P2-004.title: Perplexity acquires Read.cv, a social media platform for professionals
P2-004.author: TechCrunch
P2-004.excerpt: Read.cv launched 2021 as 'sleek alternative to LinkedIn' built around designer/creator communities. Founder Tina Roth Eisenberg's design-focused community. Acquired by Perplexity Jan 2025; team moves to Perplexity. Users given until May 16, 2025 to export data. Read.cv shut down as a platform. Official statement: 'We've long admired Perplexity and believe great things happen when the world's knowledge is made more open and accessible. This is incredibly bittersweet for us, as the start of this new chapter will mark the end of our time with @read_cv.' Notable user reaction: 'Users are increasingly treated like stepping stools for founders... users help grow a product with their network, their content, or their money, get comfortable with that product, integrate it into their lives... and then it's yanked out from under them.'
P2-004.relevance: Confirms the 'beloved niche LinkedIn alternative cannot reach scale' pattern; informs user trust crisis when platforms acqui-hire.
P2-004.credibility: high — first-party news on a public acquisition.

P2-005.title: Polywork Shuts Down (HN discussion)
P2-005.author: Multiple commenters
P2-005.excerpt: TheCapeGreek: 'Tried it when it was a LinkedIn competitor. There's one thing that I notice with these sorts of startups that take on larger social network apps. If you're not in the US and you try it, chances are you'll find very few users from your own region. For some platforms that's fine, you can find plenty of others to interact with online where location isn't that big an issue (eg BlueSky). But for something like a professional network it's different. You're very likely building your career network based on who you actually know, which is often starting from your area or country. That makes something like Polywork pretty pointless early on.' ShaggyHotDog: 'I actually liked their UI out of the box, but seems like they started as LinkedIn competitor but ended up becoming a website builder.'
P2-005.relevance: Direct user voice explaining the network-effect / regional-cold-start problem for any LinkedIn alternative.
P2-005.credibility: medium — anonymous HN user but specific lived-experience comment, internally coherent.

P2-006.title: Read CV Acquired by Perplexity (HN discussion)
P2-006.author: Multiple HN users (JadoJodo, pixeldrifter, tobr, elashri, dorian-graph, andresmax, yawnxyz)
P2-006.excerpt: JadoJodo: 'Users help grow a product...and then it's yanked out from under them.' pixeldrifter: 'This feels like a betrayal. Specifically, the focus on cashing out your idea, which overtakes the desire to make your product the best it can be.' tobr: 'Acquhired. Read.cv and posts.cv are shutting down on a rather aggressive schedule.' elashri: 'To avoid relying on projects like that in the future. I use selfhosted version of Reactive Resume.' dorian-graph: 'Yet again, let down by a centralised platform...most of the ones I haven't been disappointed by are forums, as many have lasted 10+ years.' yawnxyz: 'Maybe the project wasn't sustainable and the team decided to move on?'
P2-006.relevance: Multi-voice evidence of user trust crisis after centralized platform acquisitions; pull toward self-hosting and durable-platform skepticism.
P2-006.credibility: medium-high — multiple independent named HN users, dated discussion.

P2-007.title: Eulogy for a product that really cared: read.cv and posts.cv say goodbye
P2-007.author: Facundo Matiaude (designer)
P2-007.excerpt: 'Read.cv is a show, don't tell professional network with beautiful profiles and meaningful connections.' 'It never felt overwhelming. Just the right amount of interaction, like a conversation instead of a broadcast.' 'They built a culture of trust — where quality mattered more than quantity, by setting a precedent for warmth and care.' 'They proved that it is possible to build around heart and community, and hopes aren't lost for crafting authentic, organic spaces.' Author describes himself as in the 'denial stage of grief'; reports community-wide 'memes of digital grief' and discussions of migrating to Bluesky.
P2-007.relevance: Best qualitative articulation of what a non-LinkedIn professional network should feel like: work-evidence > credentials, conversation > broadcast, quality > quantity.
P2-007.credibility: high — named designer, dated, articulate, representative of the wider community reaction.

P2-008.title: One-Quarter of Jobs Posted Online Are Fake Ghost Jobs
P2-008.author: Entrepreneur (covering ResumeUp.AI study)
P2-008.excerpt: 27.4% of all U.S. job listings on LinkedIn are likely ghost jobs. Los Angeles 30.5%, Philadelphia 30.1%, NYC 26.7%, SF 26.0%. International: Canada 24.9%, UK 14.2%, Australia 10.9%. Average time-to-fill: 41 days in 2024. Congressional Research Service definition: 'online job postings for positions that do not exist, or that employers are not planning to fill immediately.' Employer motivations per HR survey: 43% want to project growth, 62% want to make current employees feel replaceable, 38% maintain board presence, 36% test job descriptions, 26% build talent pools.
P2-008.relevance: Primary quantitative evidence for the 'ghost jobs' pain point that erodes trust in LinkedIn as a job-search platform.
P2-008.credibility: high — methodology disclosed, multiple independent outlets reported the ResumeUp.AI study, congressional source for definition.

P2-009.title: Ghost Jobs Exposed: The Companies Posting Fake Job Listings
P2-009.author: The Interview Guys
P2-009.excerpt: Companies named as ghost-job offenders include Accenture ('positions that stay open for months without any hiring activity'), CVS Health ('positions that never seem to get filled despite thousands of applications'), and Dice ('accused of hosting or posting positions that lead nowhere'). Direct paralegal-graduate quote: 'Over a year later, I'm working as a cashier to make ends meet, still trying to land my first professional role. Despite sending out thousands of applications, the silence I've received is deafening.' 45% of HR pros 'regularly' post ghost jobs, 48% 'occasionally'; combined 93% engage in ghost-job posting to some degree. 75% of applicants get no response (Human Capital Institute). 81% of job seekers feel stressed about finances during search (Aerotek).
P2-009.relevance: Concrete user voice on the lived consequences of ghost jobs + corroborates ResumeUp.AI macro stat.
P2-009.credibility: medium — career-content site, but the company names and statistics are independently verifiable.

P2-010.title: LinkedIn Engagement Down 50%: Algorithm Insights Report 2025
P2-010.author: Writtenly Hub (covering Richard van der Blom's Algorithm Insights Report)
P2-010.excerpt: Underlying study analyzed 1.8M posts. Organic views down 50% YoY, engagement down 25%, follower growth down 59%. Company page reach 1.6% of followers (down 15% from late 2023). Personal profiles get 561% more reach than company pages. Company content now 1-2% of feed (was 7% in 2021). Native vertical video <60s: +69% performance boost. Text-only company-page posts: 0.28 multiplier (heavily penalized). A marketing director saw her post reach drop from 8,500 impressions to 340 overnight in April after the engagement-pod crackdown. LinkedIn chief editor Daniel Roth: 'LinkedIn is intentionally helping content reach more relevant audiences rather than larger audiences.' LinkedIn VP of Product: 'Our goal is to make engagement pods entirely ineffective.' New 360Brew AI (150B params) detects AI-generated text and de-prioritizes it.
P2-010.relevance: First-party quantitative evidence that LinkedIn organic reach is structurally collapsing — and that LinkedIn is actively fighting the slop, partially fixing the very pain point users complain about.
P2-010.credibility: high — underlying study (Van der Blom) is the industry-standard LinkedIn algorithm report.

P2-011.title: LinkedIn has become an obsession of top corporate brass. What gives?
P2-011.author: Sherwood News
P2-011.excerpt: John Hickey, BestOfLinkedIn creator: 'They are abusing this copy-and-paste strategy of these tall tales of business that are supposed to make them stand out, but all they are actually doing is becoming more of the same.' Joe Jaffe, Perception marketing exec: 'Marketing with decent reach disguised as thought leadership.' Avi Goren, Marqii CEO: 'Even in B2B, our restaurant partners don't want to buy from a business. They want to buy from another human.' Olivia Walch, Arcascope CEO: 'If this is advancing the goals of your company, keep doing it. If you are doing it just to fit in...you're just wasting time.' LinkedIn reports 23% increase in CEO posts since January 2024, follower engagement up 31%.
P2-011.relevance: Named-executive on-record quotes about LinkedIn's performative culture — distinct from anonymous Reddit complaints.
P2-011.credibility: high — business reporting with multiple named, identifiable sources.

P2-012.title: LinkedIn 'Broetry': Why I Hate the Algorithm-Baiting Era
P2-012.author: Peter Bencsik (innovation consultant)
P2-012.excerpt: 'We are no longer writing for humans; we are writing for machines.' 'Turning a 50-word thought into a vertically stretched poem, creators force you to spend more time scrolling.' Rewards 'engagement hacking over actual expertise' and 'effectively kills nuance.' Posts deliberately hide context behind 'See more' buttons to trigger algorithm engagement signals rather than serving readers genuinely. 'Treats the reader like a dopamine-addicted toddler.' Notable irony: contrasts LinkedIn's preference for brevity with the 2025 Nobel Prize in Literature awarded to László Krasznahorkai, celebrated for lengthy complex sentences.
P2-012.relevance: Articulate single-author take on broetry / algorithmic culture problem.
P2-012.credibility: medium — single named consultant, dated; opinion piece but argumentation is concrete.

P2-013.title: Why LinkedIn Sucks
P2-013.author: The Startup Guy (Startup Synergy Substack)
P2-013.excerpt: Five core complaints: (1) Brand identity crisis — perceived as 'cringe' and fake; (2) Performance of intelligence over authenticity; (3) Algorithm failures promoting low-effort and AI-generated content; (4) Cultural degradation into 'hustle porn, humble brags, lessons from everything, fake announcements'; (5) Misaligned features — '#OPENTOWORK' tags and skill endorsements 'pointless.' Verbatim: 'The smarter you try to be, the dumber you look; and the more you try to impress others, the less impressive you become.' 'Content wouldn't be a problem if LinkedIn's algorithm weren't promoting them...The platform is in love with crap.' 'Professionals stayed because they had to, not because they wanted to.'
P2-013.relevance: Synthesizes the LinkedIn-skeptic worldview; the closing line — 'stayed because they had to' — is the most precise statement of the migration paradox.
P2-013.credibility: medium — pseudonymous Substack author, but the arguments are widely echoed and the writing is coherent.

P2-014.title: On LinkedIn, 220 million people are 'open to work.' Recruiters weigh in if the feature helps or hurts job seekers
P2-014.author: CNBC
P2-014.excerpt: 220 million users have 'Open to Work' enabled. Recruiter views are split. Positive: using the feature doubles chances of recruiter outreach; public green badge increases that by ~40%. Many recruiters report viewing the banner neutrally or positively as a clear availability signal. Negative: Former Google recruiter Nolan Church called it 'the biggest red flag a job candidate can have' because it 'makes a job seeker appear desperate.' Context: mass tech layoffs 2023-2025 normalized the badge; senior executives at major companies openly use it.
P2-014.relevance: Concrete stats on the Open-to-Work badge as a recurring pain/identity-stigma issue plus a counterweight that stigma is fading.
P2-014.credibility: high — major business outlet, named recruiter source.

P2-015.title: 10 LinkedIn Success Stories
P2-015.author: Remote-First Coaching
P2-015.excerpt: Jennifer Dills (Microsoft Services Events Manager): 'recruiters were finding ME and contacting me about jobs they had available.' Naresh Vissa (media): hired after a publisher director DM'd: 'I found your profile on LinkedIn and wanted to reach out...discuss an opportunity.' Judi Wunderlich (WunderLand Group founder): posted about job loss → next-day InMail from staffing firm president's brother → started her own recruiting company two months later. Michael Raanan (Landmark Tax Group): joined 53 LinkedIn groups, created own 'California Business Owners' group (145 members) → established credibility, runs successful tax practice.
P2-015.relevance: Counterweight evidence — silent majority who got real career value from LinkedIn. Older (2014) but still representative pattern; corroborated by 2025 testimonials elsewhere.
P2-015.credibility: low-medium — first-party named individuals but obvious selection bias (coaching site).

P2-016.title: LinkedIn for Lawyers: Expand Your Reach
P2-016.author: GrowLaw (legal marketing firm, citing ABA + HubSpot)
P2-016.excerpt: 'LinkedIn is one of the most effective platforms for lawyers to build authority, expand their network, and attract high-quality clients in 2026.' 90% of attorneys are active on LinkedIn (ABA). LinkedIn is 277% more effective at generating leads than Facebook and Twitter (HubSpot). 150M+ U.S. professionals on platform. Clio 2024 Legal Trends Report: 40%+ of legal professionals rely on LinkedIn for networking. Clio 2025: nearly 60% of clients pick lawyers based on trusted referrals — and LinkedIn is the channel through which warm referrals are built.
P2-016.relevance: Non-creative, non-tech professional segment for which LinkedIn is structurally indispensable. Crucial counterweight to designer/dev complaints.
P2-016.credibility: medium-high — secondary source aggregating ABA + HubSpot + Clio first-party stats.

P2-017.title: The Ultimate Guide to LinkedIn B2B Marketing: 2026 Social Selling Strategies
P2-017.author: Brands at Play (B2B marketing agency)
P2-017.excerpt: 70% of B2B marketers view LinkedIn as the most effective platform for lead generation. 80% of B2B social leads originate on LinkedIn. Sales professionals who actively practice social selling are 45% more likely to meet or exceed their sales quotas. Top-performing salespeople 51% more likely to reach quota when using social selling. Inbound leads from authority-based social selling close at 14.6% vs. 1.7% for outbound prospecting.
P2-017.relevance: Quantifies the irreplaceability of LinkedIn for B2B sales — a major non-creative segment that anchors LinkedIn's moat.
P2-017.credibility: medium — agency content but citing widely-corroborated industry stats.

P2-018.title: LinkedIn Talent Solutions / Recruiter product
P2-018.author: LinkedIn (first-party) + GoBeyond 2025 recruiter survey + SHRM
P2-018.excerpt: 87% of recruiters still rely on LinkedIn for sourcing (GoBeyond 2025 recruiter survey). LinkedIn Recruiter provides access to 1B+ professionals, AI-Assisted Search, 40+ advanced search features, 100-150 InMails/month. LinkedIn Hiring Assistant (announced Oct 2024, globally available Sep 2025) saves 4 hours per role, reviews 62% fewer profiles, achieves 69% higher InMail acceptance. SHRM 2025 Talent Trends: 89% of HR pros using AI say it saves time. LinkedIn Future of Recruiting 2025: gen-AI users recover ~20% of work week (~1 full workday).
P2-018.relevance: Confirms the recruiter-side moat. Any candidate-identity product cannot bypass the fact that recruiters live in LinkedIn Recruiter.
P2-018.credibility: medium-high — first-party LinkedIn product page + third-party SHRM survey.

P2-019.title: Goodbye Bento — designer reaction to shutdown
P2-019.author: Chi Señires (designer)
P2-019.excerpt: 'Just got the email that Bento is sunsetting soon and that it'll shut down on February 13, 2026, so now I've just made an update to remove the link in my Contact page and wherever else I set it up. It was a nice 3 years of having it up, since the visual widgets were cool, but now I guess I don't have a need for another linktree.' 'I still have omg.lol which works more or less the same 😆' She exported her data and contemplates the future: 'I'll just see in the future if I do anything with that information. Maybe re-set up the places I've visited section.' She screenshotted the page before shutdown to preserve it.
P2-019.relevance: Real designer's resigned reaction to a third LinkedIn-adjacent identity platform dying — and her durable answer is a personal website (omg.lol), not another centralized platform.
P2-019.credibility: high — named designer's first-person blog, dated.

P2-020.title: Bento to shut down in 2026 as Linktree takes over and offers migration path
P2-020.author: AlternativeTo
P2-020.excerpt: Bento.me announced shutdown for February 13, 2026, more than two years after being acquired by Linktree. All user data and content removed after this date; existing Bento links redirect to Linktree. Linktree previously did the same thing in early 2024 after acquiring another product. Bento differentiated by grid-based content placement and flexible block sizing. Users encouraged to export their content before Feb 13.
P2-020.relevance: Confirms the third platform shutdown in 14 months — Polywork (Dec 2024) → Read.cv (May 2025) → Bento.me (Feb 2026).

P2-021.title: Is Substack Becoming the Next LinkedIn 2.0?
P2-021.author: Wes Pearce (Escape The Cubicle, Substack)
P2-021.excerpt: 'Depth beats breadth. That consistency beats virality. That authenticity beats authority.' 'The content that goes viral on LinkedIn feels hollow on Substack.' 'The engagement tactics that work on other platforms feel manipulative here. The audience that follows you for quick tips and motivational content doesn't stick around for long-form, thoughtful writing.' Worry that prominent personalities are 'bringing the same LinkedIn energy' to Substack. Encourages writers: readers 'get tired of it. Fast' when facing recycled influencer content.
P2-021.relevance: Articulates why writers/thought-leaders are migrating to Substack — and what they specifically value (depth, ownership, no broetry).
P2-021.credibility: medium — single Substack author opinion, but echoes a widely-observed migration pattern.

P2-022.title: Contra Reviews (Product Hunt)
P2-022.author: Multiple Product Hunt reviewers (aggregated)
P2-022.excerpt: Strengths: combines portfolio presentation with freelance operations in one clean place. 'Whether you're a designer, developer, writer, or any other creative professional, Contra.com provides the canvas you need.' No commissions. Some freelancers report ~$50k earned through Contra with higher rates than other platforms. Weaknesses: 'slow, confusing approval process, required portfolio videos, long waits, unclear rejections, and fewer opportunities than expected.' Biggest criticism: not enough jobs — 'on Fiverr and Upwork, you pay for access, but you're also paying for an audience, while on Contra, you need to bring your own.' Many designers use Contra alongside LinkedIn, not instead of it.
P2-022.relevance: Real-user reviews of the leading designer-side LinkedIn alternative. Confirms the structural problem: even loved alternatives can't deliver job-volume without the LinkedIn-scale demand side.
P2-022.credibility: medium — aggregated user reviews; specific complaints are corroborated across G2 and Trustpilot.

P2-023.title: Personal Website vs. LinkedIn: When You Need Both
P2-023.author: CareerBldr
P2-023.excerpt: 'When your LinkedIn profile reflects your old career and you're building credibility in a new one, a personal website lets you present your new direction front and center — without the baggage of your existing professional history.' On your personal website, you can host detailed case studies with screenshots, code snippets, and a story about the challenges you faced. In contrast, LinkedIn portfolio has very limited customization and is not ideal for deep case studies. Synthesis: 'LinkedIn is for being found. Your website is for proving you're worth keeping.'
P2-023.relevance: Articulates the most common alternative behavior: keep LinkedIn for discoverability + personal site for substance. Identifies the 'career changer' as the highest-pain user.
P2-023.credibility: medium — career-content site, but synthesizes a well-documented behavior pattern.

P2-024.title: Notion Portfolio Templates
P2-024.author: Notion
P2-024.excerpt: Notion offers customizable templates for designers, writers, artists, AI/ML specialists, data scientists, and software engineers seeking a structured and professional portfolio. Features include: portfolio page, resume integration, dashboard for easy setup, automated contact form, About me page that humanizes the brand. Templates emphasize customizability, organization for easy navigation, and integration with other tools.
P2-024.relevance: Documents the Notion-as-portfolio behavior — relevant because Notion is increasingly the default 'I want to be more than my LinkedIn' tool for AI-fluent professionals.

P2-025.title: How to Use Social Selling to Drive B2B Sales on LinkedIn
P2-025.author: Sales and Marketing Magazine
P2-025.excerpt: Social selling has grown into a holistic strategy that extends far beyond simply connecting — today it means using digital platforms not only to connect but to provide value, foster trust, demonstrate expertise, and maintain top-of-mind awareness. Modern B2B social selling now incorporates video content, interactive posts, direct messaging with AI enhancements, value-rich newsletters, and participation in expert forums. LinkedIn now uses advanced AI not only to recommend content but to assist sellers in researching accounts, crafting personalized outreach, and identifying possible buying signals based on subtle profile changes.
P2-025.relevance: Industry view on how LinkedIn is doubling down on B2B sales workflows — making it structurally harder for any alternative to compete on the recruiter/seller side.

P2-026.title: 'It's a miraculous solution for recruiters': LinkedIn Hiring Assistant redefines recruitment in 2026
P2-026.author: HRD Asia
P2-026.excerpt: LinkedIn Hiring Assistant — LinkedIn's first AI agent for recruiters — announced October 2024 and globally available in English since end of September 2025. Automates intake, sourcing, pre-screening, and message drafting. Early adopters save 4 hours per role, review 62% fewer profiles, and see 69% improvement in InMail acceptance rates. Quote: 'It's a miraculous solution for recruiters.'
P2-026.relevance: LinkedIn is deepening its recruiter-side moat with AI — strengthens the case that the migration window is closing on the demand side.
P2-026.credibility: high — HR trade publication, dated, specific product.

P2-027.title: LinkedIn for Finance and Accounting Leaders: Building Credibility and Influence
P2-027.author: Robert Half (recruiting firm)
P2-027.excerpt: 'LinkedIn is no longer just a digital resume. A well-positioned LinkedIn profile is where professionals can demonstrate leadership, share insights and attract talent.' Best practices for finance leaders: keep profile current with roles, certifications, degrees, projects; engage with industry content; share insights; ask for recommendations; use 'Open to Work' feature. Finance leaders who engage even once or twice weekly stay top of mind. LinkedIn groups like 'Accountants of the Future' and 'Accounting | Finance | Professionals' provide industry discussion spaces.
P2-027.relevance: Confirms LinkedIn is essential for finance/accounting — another non-creative segment with high LinkedIn dependency and low migration appetite.
P2-027.credibility: medium — recruiting firm content, but Robert Half is one of the largest accounting/finance staffing firms with first-hand recruiter knowledge.

P2-028.title: Job Hunting: Is LinkedIn's 'Open to Work' Badge Your Secret Weapon?
P2-028.author: Design Gurus
P2-028.excerpt: 'Some people might think those with the Open to Work badge are unemployed or desperate, though this stigma can exist even if it isn't always true.' Effectiveness data: using the feature doubles chances of recruiter message; public green badge increases that by 40%. Verdict: stigma persists but is fading post-2023-2025 layoffs as senior executives also use the badge openly.
P2-028.relevance: Documents the Open-to-Work stigma pain point and shows it's a milder pain than 'broken algorithm' or 'ghost jobs.'
P2-028.credibility: medium — coaching site, but stat is widely cited.

P2-029.title: Everything I posted on LinkedIn leading up to launch. A deep dive.
P2-029.author: Indie Hackers user
P2-029.excerpt: An indie hacker started posting on LinkedIn 3 months before launch, then on Product Hunt 2 months before, and focused on consistency by posting 1-2 times per week and engaging with others to stay top of mind. One company's LinkedIn job post produced 248 apply clicks. LinkedIn used effectively as a distribution channel for indie product launches.
P2-029.relevance: Indie/founder counter-narrative: LinkedIn is a working distribution channel even for the most LinkedIn-skeptical cohort (indie hackers).
P2-029.credibility: medium — user-posted account on Indie Hackers, internal coherence good.

P2-030.title: Damon Chen: From Engineer to Indie Hacker Making $1.3M ARR
P2-030.author: Creator Economy newsletter
P2-030.excerpt: Damon Chen built Testimonial.to to $1.3M ARR mostly solo. Leveraged LinkedIn as a distribution channel for B2B SaaS targeting marketers and creators. Confirms LinkedIn's effectiveness for B2B-targeted indie founders — even those who otherwise reject 'LinkedIn culture.'
P2-030.relevance: High-credibility case study of LinkedIn distribution working for indie B2B founders.
P2-030.credibility: medium-high — named founder, verifiable revenue claims, well-known in indie hacker community.

P2-031.title: Perplexity Acquires Professional Networking Platform Read.cv
P2-031.author: Cosmico
P2-031.excerpt: Many Read.cv users expressed dissatisfaction over the impending shutdown, with concerns raised about data migration and the loss of unique platform features. 'When the news came out, people were writing saddened posts, trying to create groups to replicate posts.cv, publishing memes of digital grief, coming up with weird ideas to buy out the platform and trying to choose whether to migrate the community to Bluesky or some other low key social platform.' One designer noted frustration that 'the industry doesn't allow platforms like Read.cv to keep existing while the team works for Perplexity.'
P2-031.relevance: Captures the wider community grief and migration discussion after Read.cv shutdown.
P2-031.credibility: medium — secondary news source aggregating community reactions.

P2-032.title: R.I.P. Read.CV, former LinkedIn Competitor
P2-032.author: Patricia Parnet (Medium)
P2-032.excerpt: Designer reflection on Read.cv being acquired by Perplexity and shut down. Reinforces themes: bittersweet farewell, community grief, frustration that beloved niche product couldn't survive independently, search for next home (Bluesky, omg.lol, self-hosted).
P2-032.relevance: Additional designer voice on Read.cv shutdown — corroborates Matiaude eulogy.
P2-032.credibility: medium — Medium opinion post, dated, identified author.

P2-033.title: 10 Best Contra Alternatives (Twine Blog)
P2-033.author: Twine
P2-033.excerpt: After six to eight months of working through platforms like Upwork, the bulk of quality client leads shift to LinkedIn for many freelancers, with one user sharing that by simply sending out connection requests and keeping an eye on job postings, recruiters eventually begin to reach out. Use Contra alongside other platforms, not instead of them. For designers, Contra works best if you already have an audience or existing client base to leverage.
P2-033.relevance: Confirms that even freelance-platform users return to LinkedIn for high-quality leads — LinkedIn's hold over the demand side persists.
P2-033.credibility: medium — content marketing site, but the freelancer behavior pattern is widely echoed.

P2-034.title: LinkedIn Algorithm 2026: How Reach, Engagement, and Visibility Work Now
P2-034.author: designACE
P2-034.excerpt: LinkedIn has restructured its algorithm to prioritize relevance over reach. Introduced 360Brew (150B-param AI model) that 'reads your content the way a human editor would.' Reasons for reach decline: more people are posting; ads take up more space; algorithm now cares more about saves and comment quality than raw likes. Native video saw a 69% performance boost, especially vertical mobile-first videos under 60 seconds. LinkedIn's AI is now smart enough to detect AI-generated content, and it's not rewarding it. Employee advocacy now drives 30% of total company engagement despite only 3% of employees sharing company content.
P2-034.relevance: Confirms LinkedIn is fighting the AI-slop / engagement-bait problem at the algorithm level — partially defusing the cultural critique.
P2-034.credibility: medium — agency blog, but corroborates Writtenly Hub and Van der Blom data.

P2-035.title: Bento.me shutdown announcement (AlternativeTo on X)
P2-035.author: AlternativeTo (verified X account)
P2-035.excerpt: 'The one page links hub @joinbento will shut down on February 13, 2026, two years after being acquired by Linktree. Users should export their data before the shutdown, as Bento links will redirect to Linktree.'
P2-035.relevance: Public announcement record of the Bento shutdown — anchor fact for the three-platforms-in-14-months pattern.

P2-036.title: Why LinkedIn Is Just a Cringe Fest for Wannabe Thought Leaders
P2-036.author: Usman Writes (Medium)
P2-036.excerpt: Users mimic the same buzzwords like 'thought leader' and 'team-player,' or other corporate jargon that feels like it is used just to fit a mold. Long, overwrought captions, performative posts, and algorithm-chasing content have made 'LinkedIn cringe' its own genre. Posts deliberately hide context behind 'See more' buttons to trigger algorithm engagement signals rather than serving readers genuinely.
P2-036.relevance: Aggregates the broetry / thought-leader cringe complaint in user voice.
P2-036.credibility: low-medium — Medium opinion post, but representative of a widely-echoed view.

P2-037.title: Ryan Law: 'Thought leadership' is cringe-inducing (LinkedIn post)
P2-037.author: Ryan Law (LinkedIn post, 44 comments)
P2-037.excerpt: 'Thought leadership' is a cringe-inducing topic with a serious branding problem. To claim you have 'leading thoughts' is the highest order of narcissism. Even if the epithet was accurate, who would I be following such leading thoughts?
P2-037.relevance: On-LinkedIn meta-criticism of LinkedIn culture — proves the cringe-frame is acknowledged even by LinkedIn-native creators.

P2-038.title: LinkedIn's Algorithm in 2025: Why Engagement Pods Are Dead
P2-038.author: Synergist Digital Media on DEV.to
P2-038.excerpt: LinkedIn explicitly killed engagement pods. A marketing director saw her average post reach drop from 8,500 impressions to 340 overnight in April because she was in three engagement pods. LinkedIn VP of Product: 'Our goal is to make engagement pods entirely ineffective.' Recommendations: ditch growth hacks, post native vertical video <60s, focus on saves and comment quality.
P2-038.relevance: Concrete worked example of how LinkedIn algorithm changes are punishing the very 'broetry/engagement-pod' content people complained about.
P2-038.credibility: medium-high — practitioner reporting on observed algorithm shift, dated.

P2-039.title: How to identify a ghost job, according to LinkedIn's head of career products
P2-039.author: Fast Company
P2-039.excerpt: LinkedIn confirmed that ghost job complaints increased in 2025, with the platform's internal research showing that one in three job seekers had experienced fake or inactive listings. LinkedIn published guidance on how to identify a ghost job — implicitly acknowledging the problem at the platform level.
P2-039.relevance: LinkedIn's own admission of the ghost-jobs problem — strongest possible source for the pain point being real.
P2-039.credibility: high — major business outlet with LinkedIn executive on record.

P2-040.title: Has LinkedIn helped you get a job? Yes, 4 major breakthroughs
P2-040.author: Anshul Agarwal (LinkedIn Pulse)
P2-040.excerpt: Personal account of four career breakthroughs initiated through LinkedIn connections / direct messages / job applications. Representative of the silent-majority who use LinkedIn pragmatically and find value, without complaining.
P2-040.relevance: Silent-majority counterweight to the public complaint wave.
P2-040.credibility: low — single LinkedIn post, no independent verification, but representative pattern.

P2-041.title: Networking Strategies for Lawyers: Turn Professional Connections into Leads
P2-041.author: American Bar Association
P2-041.excerpt: Trusted recommendations remain the leading source of new legal work with nearly 60% of clients choosing lawyers based on referrals they have confidence in. Quality connections outperform quantity every time. Online networking for legal professionals is not just about adding contacts but about building trust that leads to meaningful referrals.
P2-041.relevance: ABA-first-party confirmation that LinkedIn-mediated trust-building remains structurally essential to legal professionals.

P2-042.title: Why LinkedIn feels so cringey right now
P2-042.author: MTDeco (Substack)
P2-042.excerpt: Detailed creator-economy analysis of why LinkedIn content quality has declined: incentive structures reward performance over substance, AI tools have flooded the system, executive participation has shifted the tone toward inauthentic 'thought leadership.'
P2-042.relevance: Additional voice on LinkedIn cringe / inauthenticity culture.
P2-042.credibility: medium — Substack post, but argument is well-developed.

P2-043.title: Proof of Work Portfolios: Real Examples That Open Doors
P2-043.author: Fueler
P2-043.excerpt: 'Proof of work portfolios beat resumes because they demonstrate skills instantly while CVs make claims.' Personal-website case studies with screenshots, code snippets, and challenge narratives outperform LinkedIn portfolio sections for career changers and creative professionals. 'LinkedIn is for being found. Your website is for proving you're worth keeping.'
P2-043.relevance: Articulates the 'works-evidence' thesis — supports our insight that AI-era careers will be portfolio-led, not credential-led.
P2-043.credibility: medium — startup blog (Fueler is a portfolio product so has interest), but the worked-examples pattern is widely echoed.

P2-044.title: 4 Startup Lessons from Indie Hackers
P2-044.author: Thắm Nguyễn (LinkedIn Pulse)
P2-044.excerpt: Reflection on indie-hacker / founder culture that increasingly bypasses LinkedIn-style credential signaling in favor of public-build, Build-in-Public, and direct distribution via X/Substack/personal site.
P2-044.relevance: Indie-hacker founder voice on alternative-to-LinkedIn identity-building.

P2-045.title: Recruiters weigh in if LinkedIn's Open to Work helps or hurts (CNBC)
P2-045.author: CNBC
P2-045.excerpt: Headline number: 220 million people have Open-to-Work enabled. Nolan Church (former Google recruiter, now CEO): the badge is 'the biggest red flag a job candidate can have' because 'it makes a job seeker appear desperate.' Counter view: most recruiters view it neutrally; stigma effectively dissolved after mass 2023-2025 tech layoffs.
P2-045.relevance: Most-cited stat on Open-to-Work badge plus the named recruiter quote that frames the stigma issue.
